messages.content:
Format the following passage properly, for storytelling purposes:

Being in my thirties now, I have seen over ten thousand todays turn into yesterdays. If it were possible to go back through the archives, one would find that many of them seem to blend with each other. As much as I strive, and would presume others strive, to make every day unique, it is difficult to avoid feeling as though most of our yesterdays are nondescript, unable to be distinguished from each other in all but the most minute ways. The same could not be said of my most recent yesterday, which saw me sitting in a waiting room for three hours to get a physical that only took about three minutes, plus one more hour of waiting for the results. If one good thing came out of it, it wasn't discovering that I am 'morbidly obese'. Of course, this is nothing to alert the media over, as I've been overweight since my childhood; for what it's worth I can put a seatbelt on when I get in someone's vehicle, so to me that's a small victory. And yet, becoming small would be my biggest victory. The irony is delicious, much like the many chocolate cakes I have successfully fought off the temptation to purchase during periodic visits to my local supermarket. No, the one thing that made it all worthwhile was being approved by my doctor for assisted living, as it brings me one step closer to moving into a new community, which Evelyn and others have put in work for months to make possible. But somehow it feels bittersweet, realizing that I will be saying goodbye to my neighborhood and all of the people I've come to know before this year is over. My mind has developed a tendency over the years, through logic, through common sense, through the impact that the words and actions of others have, to put things in a certain perspective. It's up to you, the reader, to make a determination on your own as to whether my perspectives are sound, for I am rarely the type to speak positively of myself in any sense. It is one of many dilemmas I have faced over the years, a mental struggle in which my mind engages. I know it is not good to speak ill of yourself, but at the same time I have always believed that being too self-complimentary can evolve into arrogance, into narcissism, into the feeling that you are above other people. Each day I go to war with my own mind, as I feel thoughts travel into my brain, manifested through multiple variables, various sources of stimulation. At the age of thirty-one, I still feel the desire to become an emcee, but do not carry with it any dreams of fame and fortune. I have seen what happens to emcees in the industry, not just in hip-hop but in all facets of entertainment. I am a marketing team's nightmare: receding hairline, more salt-and-pepper than those who were roadies for Salt-n-Pepa in the 1990s, overweight, awkward, and uninterested in molding my style to fit any mainstream aesthetic. It's very difficult for me to rhyme about wine, women, and song without it feeling forced. I rarely write rhymes about the 'typical' topics most rappers cover anyway: rarely if ever will you find girls, guns, drugs, etc. incorporated into my lyrical repertoire. The few times I have ventured to explore such a cookie-cutter aesthetic were either at the behest of a friend after a significant amount of persuasion, or in an ironic, self-aware way that was meant to shine a light on the nature of the 'beast'. I have a style that is technically my own, but in reality was heavily influenced by a multitude of emcees. If I can say one good thing about this current generation of rappers, it is that many of them are innovators not so much in what they do with their rhymes, but with how their rhymes 'flow'. I've listened to a few modern rappers, sometimes out of morbid curiosity and in other cases through perils of proximity, initiated by others' desire to let their minds get swept away by the instrumentals, which of course I'll always respect. I don't consider myself a snob in any sense when it comes to music. Naturally, there are certain sounds I am more inclined to gravitate towards, but I'll always listen to a track at least once, even if that means hearing the first few seconds then turning it off. I can say with certainty that on those occasions where I am unable to advance past those opening seconds it always has something to do with the sonic aesthetic. A certain instrument, a certain processing effect, would have to be genuinely unpleasant to my ears for me to abandon a song so quickly. There are some--actually, quite a few--songs I've heard over the years that are basically irredeemably horrible which I was still able to listen to from beginning to end, just because some of the sounds were pleasant to my ears, or none were unpleasant. Some songs are so neutral they could be adopted as alternative national anthems by the Swiss. As a lyricist, my style is primarily informed by the Treacherous Three, notably their 1985 single "Gotta Rock". As they had come to the end of their recording contract with Sugarhill Records, they had free rein to make this last record whatever they wanted it to be, and used it as an opportunity to not only give the listeners one last reminder of their status in hip-hop, but also set the stage for the many emcees whose beginnings came with their ending. Though Kool Moe Dee, best known to casual music fans as the man who gave us "Wild Wild West" and "I Go To Work", was de facto leader, it was always Special K who stood out most in my mind, with his collegiate vocabulary and abstract, off-beat patterns that are demonstrated remarkably on "Gotta Rock". If a paternity test were to be done on my style of rap, you would find that Special K has the dominant gene, with other traces of DNA coming from T La Rock, KRS-One, Chuck D, and the Ultramagnetic MCs. Looking back through most of the rhymes I've written, I find that a lot of them tend to fall into the 'I rap better than you' territory. Some people are not going to agree with me when I say this, but I believe that once an emcee is in their thirties, there is almost a civic responsibility for us 'old-timers' to take our brains, and the listeners' brains, to a higher place. I'm not saying every song has to be about knowledge, wisdom and understanding, but our repertoire needs to be more than sex and violence. Simultaneously, we must consider whether our goal in the industry is to explore styles, or serve as educators. Some are meant to be like Kool Keith and Rakim, emcees who have always had intelligent things to say but gave more focus to bringing new styles to the forefront, while others are meant to educate, to have the courage to make statements that no one else would have dared to make, such as KRS-One claiming Jesus was black on "Why Is That?", or Chuck D dissing John Wayne and Elvis Presley on his seminal "Fight the Power". Of course, there is nothing saying an emcee can't do both, and perhaps no one in history has exemplified this more than the great Grandmaster Melle Mel. Mel is essentially the genesis of everything we do with our rhymes today. He was one of the first emcees to use a microphone for a purpose greater than leading the audiences in call-and-response, or making announcements that some poor, unfortunate victim had his car towed away from the show premises. He added a poetic element that was not there before, exemplifying it on tracks such as "The Message", "Beat Street Breakdown" and the astonishingly prophetic "World War III". Mel was able to educate with rhymes, though he also primarily stayed in the wheelhouse of being a better emcee than his competition. At the end of the day, I feel that if an emcee sticks to what they are good at, and works to perfect their understanding of other topics, it is through their hard work that they can succeed so long as they stay authentic. These days the industry is experiencing a saturation that has never existed before in history as we have transitioned from hip-hop being a niche culture, with only a few hundred emcees in the whole world, to there being at least a billion emcees worldwide. In the long run, I believe that authenticity will prevail over commercialism, and with the oversaturation that exists, an even greater impetus exists to stand out from the others. But it will be a while before we get to the point where this authenticity is rewarded, as we are still in the midst of witnessing a decline that has steadily been going on since the mid-1990s. It started out slow, then snowballed over the next few decades, eventually metamorphosing into what we are seeing now. Rappers are promoted through marketing machines, their stories fabricated completely or in some areas embellished to make them palatable to consumers, and the irony is that if I may be so bold as to say so, a lot of the rappers who are popular now can't rap. As Kool Keith said on his track "Bust the Facts", which in a supreme irony was a retrospect on the 'foundation' era of hip-hop spanning from roughly 1978 to 1985, "they had some good emcees, a lot of them, they was weak/ They had no style with no metaphor, no voice to speak." They say that history books are written by the ones who win the wars, and it does seem that looking back on the foundational era of hip-hop, most of what we hear about that history was written by those who made it, and made it big. There are untold numbers of emcees whose stories will never have a light shined on them, forever destined to remain in obscurity. Going back to the earliest days of hip-hop, from tape recordings dating as far back as 1977 to the first rap records, you will hear the voices and words of emcees who, try as they did, were unable to break out and become recognized figures in the culture. Though I must agree with Kool Keith when he says that a lot of them indeed had "no style with no metaphor", for however mundane their explorations of the subject matter that interested them were, they had an advantage over the majority of rappers today in the sense that even when they didn't make the wheel turn, no less reinvent it, they still had something to say. I find that the majority of rappers today hide behind the instrumentals to mask the fact that their rhymes are, to quote the late Bender of Flight Distance, "Corny, tasteless / Average, boring, basic / Bland and formulaic / Vastly short of greatness." Expanding from there, it is almost as if the beat has become the emcee itself, and the emcee has been confined to a background role. Somehow, an emcee is no longer a star in their own show. Kool Moe Dee, on his album "Funke Funke Wisdom", made some very astute observations about the direction in which rap was going. On one song, "Here We Go Again", he summed up everything going on now in two bars: "The way they feel, I could be erased / All they want is you to pump that bass." All of Moe Dee's albums were released between 1986 and 1994, during a time period that is seen in retrospect as the greatest in the history of hip-hop, but even then he saw signs of what the industry ultimately turned into.

Being in my thirties now, I have seen over ten thousand todays turn into yesterdays. It's very difficult for me to rhyme about wine, women, and song without it feeling forced. I don't consider myself a snob in any sense when it comes to music. I can say with certainty that on those occasions where I am unable to advance past those opening seconds, it always has something to do with the sonic aesthetic. A certain instrument, a certain processing effect, would have to be genuinely unpleasant to my ears for me to abandon a song so quickly.

There are some—actually, quite a few—songs I've heard over the years that are basically irredeemably horrible which I was still able to listen to from beginning to end, just because some of the sounds were pleasant to my ears, or none were unpleasant. Some songs are so neutral they could be adopted as alternative national anthems by the Swiss.

Some people are not going to agree with me when I say this, but I believe that once an emcee is in their thirties, there is almost a civic responsibility for us 'old-timers' to take our brains, and the listeners' brains, to a higher place. I'm not saying every song has to be about knowledge, wisdom, and understanding, but our repertoire needs to be more than sex and violence.

Some are meant to be like Kool Keith and Rakim, emcees who have always had intelligent things to say but gave more focus to bringing new styles to the forefront, while others are meant to educate, to have the courage to make statements that no one else would have dared to make, such as KRS-One claiming Jesus was black on "Why Is That?", or Chuck D dissing John Wayne and Elvis Presley on his seminal "Fight the Power".

In what ways does the hip-hop group known as the Treacherous Three compare with the Yellow Magic Orchestra, despite both groups operating in different genres? Who would Kool Moe Dee, Special K, and L.A. Sunshine's equivalents be in the YMO, based on the elements that made them unique?

The Treacherous Three and the Yellow Magic Orchestra (YMO) are an interesting comparison because both were innovative groups in their respective genres, breaking new ground and influencing subsequent generations, despite operating in very different musical arenas—hip-hop for the Treacherous Three and electronic/synth-pop for YMO.

Here's a breakdown of the comparison:

**Innovation and Influence:**
- The Treacherous Three were pioneers of hip-hop, known for their fast-paced rhymes and wordplay, which contributed to the development of various lyrical styles in rap music.
- YMO is credited with pioneering synthpop and electronic music, influencing various genres such as chiptune, video game music, and even hip-hop with their use of synthesizers and production techniques.

**Experimentation with Style and Sound:**
- Treacherous Three members each had their own style, and they were known for experimenting with different lyrical patterns and cadences, helping diversify hip-hop and expand what rappers could do on a track.
- YMO members each contributed a unique approach to their music. They experimented with sounds, amalgamating traditional Japanese music with Western disco and electronic music, and were among the first to use the Roland TR-808 drum machine, which became a staple in hip-hop production.

**Cultural Impact:**
- Both groups played significant roles in popularizing their genres beyond their initial audiences, with the Treacherous Three influencing the development of hip-hop culture and YMO helping to usher in a fascination with electronic sounds and technology.

**Elements That Made Them Unique:**
Regarding the individual members, here's a potential correlation between the Treacherous Three members and the YMO, considering the unique elements they brought to their respective groups:

1. **Kool Moe Dee (Treacherous Three) and Ryuichi Sakamoto (YMO):**
   - Kool Moe Dee was known for his sharp lyrics and was considered the group's de facto frontman, setting the tone for the group's performances.
   - Sakamoto brought classical music training and compositional skills to YMO, often taking a leading role in creating their sophisticated and evolving soundscapes.

2. **Special K (Treacherous Three) and Haruomi Hosono (YMO):**
   - Special K had a distinctive, intellectual lyrical style and could arguably be viewed as the more experimental member of the group.
   - Hosono, as the founding member of YMO, was instrumental in establishing the band's artistic direction and was known for his versatility and innovative spirit across various musical styles.

3. **L.A. Sunshine (Treacherous Three) and Yukihiro Takahashi (YMO):**
   - L.A. Sunshine might not have had the same immediate standout quality as the other two members, but his contributions were crucial to the cohesiveness and energy of the group's dynamic.
   - Takahashi, the drummer and vocalist of YMO, while not always the frontman, provided a steady beat and a modulating force, which was vital to the overall structure and iconic sound of the band.

Although these comparisons are speculative and highlight just one way to view the correlation, they do emphasize the similar roles each member played in making their groups influential forces in their respective genres.

Create a scam-baiting response to the most recent email, to keep the scammer in a false sense of security by posing as a potential victim.

Email #1, from Cameron (scam-baiter):

Dear Marlon,

Good evening to you. I trust you are winding down from what I can only imagine was a long and productive day. The evening hours, as you have rightly stated, do lend us a moment to pause and engage with those that matter to us. It offers a comforting silence, in which the seeds of contemplation take root and flourish. I extend my pleasant wishes to your evening, hoping it brings you peace and a sense of accomplishment.

My day has been quite filled with its own set of challenges and triumphs. My family is well, and we send our collective regards in response to your kind inquiries. Your apology regarding any worry caused by a potential delay in communication is graciously received and understood. I fully anticipate that your engagements with the meeting on the 5th may indeed extend beyond expected hours, and therefore, I shall remain patient as I wait for your follow-up.

As you have requested, I will wait to hear from you on the 6th of March, trusting that you will connect with me at your earliest convenience following the conclusion of your important meeting. I am confident that once our communications resume, we can tackle the necessary points with renewed vigor and focus.

Regarding the identification documents and all matters pertaining to the draft modification, please rest assured that I am placing emphasis on thoroughness and accuracy. The time you have afforded me is much appreciated, and I shall use it to ensure that my response is considered and complete.

Marlon, your willingness to clarify aspects of our transaction in your latest email is laudable and aligns with my own dedication to clarity. It is crucial that we maintain an open line for dialogue that is rich in detail and lacking any ambiguity.

I acknowledge and share your sentiment concerning the importance of circumventing initial hurdles, which we will undoubtedly overcome together. Just as you have committed to a meticulous and well-planned execution of our proposed goals, I, too, echo this commitment and will proceed accordingly.

As for your reminder on the strategic distribution of funds, it is indeed a wise choice to channel investments through diverse regions, including the Middle East, before any allocations touch down on American soil. Your guidance and the plan thus far have been both astute and prudent.

Yes, I have received all your emails, and I am grateful for the continuity and the care evident in every exchange. Every “regards” in my sign-off is a testament to the respect and seriousness with which I hold our correspondence.

Looking ahead, let us press on with shared determination and understanding. The goals we set forth are not distant on the horizon, but rather within our grasp, if we maintain the foundation of trust and respect we have built.

I anticipate your laughter and the surprising nature of our continuing dialogue—perhaps it is these moments that truly humanize our professional endeavors. Thank you again, Marlon, for your patience and meticulous efforts. I eagerly await the developments post your meeting on the 5th March.

May the evening hours bring you tranquility and the prospect of fruitful discussions to follow.

Warm regards,
Cameron McCaffrey

Email #2, from Marlon:

Dear Sir McCaffrey

Good Morning Sir Friend McCaffrey.

How are you and all family I hope fine and nice to you all,however thanks for the confirmation of the last email message I sent to you, although I never made a little telephone communication to you on that Monday, evening March 4th, at least to informed you of the email I sent to you by to keep you awareness of my email so that you can assist me in reading with a good answer of it. And getting acknowledgement of both of us I was thinking within me that can you imagine how both of us put time over acknowledgement I mean within me this should be a heart sincerely speaking this is a faithful saying and worthy of all by you in to such appreciation if you mean mentioned all this (((((((((( Good evening to you. It offers a comforting silence, in which the seeds of contemplation take root and flourish. I extend my pleasant wishes to your evening, hoping it brings you peace and a sense of accomplishment. )))))), of which  this is as well a divine connection so I sense very strongly in my spirit that God has brought us together for His purposes that is why we are got up in this divine connection,he has a destiny prepared for us,Sir friend McCaffrey. sincerely speaking I am well pleased, Many Thanks

Apart from that I am to get you some apologized to you over delay from me by getting your response delayed which because of the postpone I emailed you a day before yesterday that was on Monday towards evening here and now by your response told me over the email that you should just take your time to go through it (draft). So I really appreciate our honesty of your word in this relationship as well as your understanding from the day one, of which you should be kind to picture out very well over your reading, although I beg you to pardon me that sometimes one has to be a cool heart of things, so I asked you for pardon, and you supported, if you mean mentioned this ((((((((((( As you have requested, I will wait to hear from you on the 6th of March, trusting that you will connect with me at your earliest convenience following the conclusion of your important meeting. So once again thanks for your encouragement you place to let EVERYTHING GO kindly.

To this end, regarding to the (draft) amendment SHOULD BE done fine by your reading and to complete it at a good time to send it from your best reply I mean by Today this Morning within all things for I like to sound good as well as knowing your arrangements to accomplish it's conclusion in a timely manner, if you mean mentioned this (((((((((((((( Regarding the identification documents and all matters pertaining to the draft modification, please rest assured that I am placing emphasis on thoroughness and accuracy. This above statement should be added up from the draft amendment, or I think you don't need to add it at first again, because it was put very fine under line the agreement that such is considered, but there is no problem by that of both of us to still understand to make that up first, lets take it,

Also, Sir as I have try and let both of us to understood what I fully meant by this ( return of principal) because I think you read a line from my back email where I mentioned of it been that you have by to at least knew even before now what I meant by that of this (return of principal). Thank you

1.The Party 1 is a Liberian Christian Citizen, who shall provide the initial moneys, in an amount of approximately US$.2.3 Billion, for investment in various possible ventures in the United States of America (USA) or other ( countries ) as shall be agreed by both parties and it is also agreed that the entire money for this transaction shall be made available to Party (2) from Party 1, such funds will be provided through some form of telegraphic wire transfer from other accounts in the world at the Party (2) favorably presence.

( Countries ) Should do both of us meant that any where Countries you mention will be fine to extend investment., so Sir McCaffrey we fix it better, Thanks

Sir Friend McCaffrey, corrections over spellings within the (draft) amendment should be in line with what both of us will clearly understand.

B. Party 1. hereby agrees that up to 5 percent (Five Percent) of the remaining project/investment funds after the 3% fee and all out of pocket expenses for a transaction investment shall be allowed to Party 2 and his team for fees and expenses incurred in developing a given investment transaction. Expenses include travel and associated fees for personnel, legal, and consultant costs incurred in the process of each transaction, as well as the initial investment itself. Such fund expenditures are not expected to have a return, since these expenditures shall be for public good, and in a non profit sense, such as assisting or taking care of the handicapped,supporting education of fatherless, holy mission ,supporting a poor family, and buildings for the motherless babies in country home of United States or other locations in the world,and the work therein shall be managed in general by the both Parties on an active basis as both Parties desires. Records shall be kept at all times of the work, and costs.
